sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 118, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 117 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 3.9x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After pressing RIGHT:
- Left feet: rows 19-23, cols 14-18 -> rows 19-23, cols 19-23 (moved right 5 cols)
- Right feet: rows 44-48, cols 44-48 -> rows 44-48, cols 39-43 (moved left 5 cols)
So the right feet were NOT blocked from moving left at cols 44-48! My earlier observation was wrong. The right feet can move between cols 39-43, 44-48, and 49-53.
Current state:
- Left feet: rows 19-23, cols 19-23 (matches initial position!)
- Right feet: rows 44-48, cols 39-43 (wrong rows and cols)
Initial state:
- Left feet: rows 19-23, cols 19-23
- Right feet: rows 54-58, cols 44-48
The left feet are now at the correct initial position! The right feet need to move to rows 54-58, cols 44-48.
To move the right feet from rows 44-48 to rows 54-58: press DOWN.
To move the right feet from cols 39-43 to cols 44-48: press RIGHT.
But pressing DOWN also moves the left feet down (from rows 19-23 to rows 29-38), and pressing RIGHT also moves the left feet right (from cols 19-23 to cols 24-28, but they might be blocked).
Wait, but earlier I observed that the left feet at cols 19-23 might be blocked from moving right. Let me check.
Actually, looking at the body shape, the left body extends from cols 9-23. So the left feet at cols 19-23 are at the right edge of the left body. If I press RIGHT, the left feet would try to move right, but they might be blocked.
Let me test this by pressing RIGHT.
But wait, I also need to move the right feet down. Let me think about the order.
If I press DOWN first:
- Left feet: rows 19-23 -> rows 29-38 (wrong rows!)
- Right feet: rows 44-48 -> rows 54-58 (correct rows!)
Then if I press RIGHT:
- Left feet: rows 29-38, cols 19-23 -> rows 29-38, cols 24-28 (but might be blocked at cols 19-23)
- Right feet: rows 54-58, cols 39-43 -> rows 54-58, cols 44-48 (correct cols!)
If the left feet are blocked from moving right at cols 19-23, then pressing RIGHT would only move the right feet right, leaving the left feet at cols 19-23. But the left feet would be at rows 29-38, not rows 19-23.
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me try pressing DOWN first to move the right feet to rows 54-58, and then see if the left feet are blocked from moving right at cols 19-23.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the problem. Maybe the goal is not to restore the initial state, but to achieve some other configuration. Let me look at the current image and see if there's any visual clue about the goal.
Looking at the image, the left feet are at rows 19-23, cols 19-23 (arm area), and the right feet are at rows 44-48, cols 39-43 (leg area). The body is a black shape with yellow and orange sections.
Maybe the goal is to have the feet at the "hands" at rows 29-38, cols 14-18 (left) and cols 49-53 (right). But I'm not sure.
Let me try pressing SPACE with the current configuration (left feet at rows 19-23, cols 19-23; right feet at rows 44-48, cols 39-43) to see if it completes the level.
Actually, let me first try to get the right feet to the initial position (rows 54-58, cols 44-48) while keeping the left feet at rows 19-23, cols 19-23.
Current state:
- Left feet: rows 19-23, cols 19-23
- Right feet: rows 44-48, cols 39-43
To move the right feet to rows 54-58, cols 44-48:
1. Press DOWN: left feet move to rows 29-38, right feet move to rows 54-58.
2. Press UP: left feet move to rows 19-23, right feet move to rows 44-48.
This doesn't help.
Actually, I think the feet move independently, and the amount they move depends on the available space. Let me try pressing DOWN once and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current feet: rows 19-23, cols 19-23 (left) and rows 44-48, cols 39-43 (right)
# Left feet at initial position. Right feet need to get to rows 54-58, cols 44-48.
# Let me try DOWN to move right feet down, and see if left feet are blocked at rows 19-23

action(['DOWN'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in node['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in node['boundary']]
        print(f"Feet: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Feet: rows 19-23, cols 19-23
Feet: rows 49-53, cols 39-43
